entry.title: Remittance Advice in SAP: What I Learned While Fixing Vendor Payment Issues
entry.content:
Why Remittance Advice Became Critical in My Project

In one of my projects, we started receiving frequent vendor complaints even though payments were being processed on time through the Automatic Payment Program (F110). Vendors were reaching out saying invoices were still showing as open on their side. After analyzing the issue, we realized the problem was not with the payment itself, but with the remittance advice. The information being sent to vendors was not clear enough for them to reconcile payments correctly.

That’s when I understood that remittance advice is not just a payment notification it plays a key role in post-payment reconciliation.

Unclear remittance advice started creating several operational challenges. Vendors were often unable to clearly identify which invoices had been paid, especially in cases where deductions like withholding tax or early payment discounts were involved. This led to payments being incorrectly applied to older or unrelated invoices.
As a result, our team began receiving a high volume of vendor queries, spending significant time investigating and clarifying payment details. During month-end, we also noticed discrepancies between vendor statements and our sub-ledger, which made reconciliation more difficult. Overall, these issues not only increased internal effort but also impacted vendor trust and communication.

To address this, I got involved in improving the remittance advice as part of an enhancement activity. While working with the F110 payment program, I first spent some time understanding how the remittance advice was being generated and where the gaps were.
I noticed that the standard output was not providing enough clarity at the invoice level, especially when deductions like tax or discounts were involved. So, I worked closely with our ABAP team to enhance the DMEE structure and include more detailed invoice-level information.
Along with that, we updated the Adobe Form layout used for remittance advice output to make the communication clearer for vendors specifically around invoice numbers, payment amounts, and deductions.
We also reviewed the vendor master data to ensure the correct communication method, such as EDI, was properly maintained so that the remittance advice consistently reached vendors in the expected format.

During this process, we also came across a few challenges:

One of the main challenges we faced was ensuring all deductions, such as tax and discounts, were clearly reflected in the remittance advice, as the standard output lacked sufficient detail. We also had issues with formatting and communication settings, which sometimes caused confusion for vendors. Overall, the challenge was making the remittance both clear and consistently delivered.

After implementing these changes, we observed:

  • Vendor queries reduced significantly
  • Vendors were able to automatically clear invoices on their side
  • Month-end reconciliation became smoother
  • Our team spent less time on manual clarification and follow-ups

This clearly showed how critical remittance advice is beyond just sending payment confirmation.

Conclusion

From my experience, remittance advice plays a crucial role in maintaining transparency between Accounts Payable and vendors. Even when payments are accurate, poor communication can create confusion and additional workload.
Getting the remittance process right, especially during implementation or enhancement, can prevent many post-go-live issues and improve overall efficiency.

entry.title: From User Assistance Developer to Agent Builder: My First Step into AI Workflows
entry.content:
I've spent years as a User Assistance Developer creating help documentation, improving clarity, and thinking from a user's perspective. Recently, I found myself asking a simple question, "Can I use AI to review documentation the way I do?"

I didn't want to become a developer. I just wanted to make my work smarter. 

That's when I started experimenting with AI tools like Claude Code. My goal was simple - build something that could read help documentation and suggest improvements from a customer's point of view.

At first, it sounded straightforward, give our SAP help portal URL, get feedback. But I quickly learned that modern websites don't always expose their content directly. Many pages load content using JavaScript, which meant I had to use a browser automation tool like Puppeteer just to see what a user sees.

What started as a small idea turned into a structured workflow.

I created an agent that:

- Reads documentation pages
- Extracts meaningful content
- Reviews it from both technical and non-technical perspectives
- Identifies what is missing, confusing, or poorly structured
- Suggests specific improvements

But the real breakthrough came when it was made efficient.

Instead of reading the website every time (which was slow), I designed it to:

- Read once and store the content
- Reuse that content for future analysis
- Only refresh when needed

This made the process fast, repeatable, and practical.

Where Claude became my developer partner

What made this journey possible was how I used Claude, not just as a chatbot, but as a fellow developer.

I didn't start with code. I started with questions.

  • How do I extract content from a webpage that loads dynamically?
  • Why isn’t this script working?
  • Can you simplify this setup for me?

Claude didn't just give answers, it helped me think through the problem step by step.

When I got stuck installing tools or setting up the environment, it guided me through it patiently. When my code didn’t work, it debugged with me. When things felt too complex, it simplified them. At one point, I realized I wasn't just asking for solutions, I was iterating with it, like I would with a teammate.

What actually surprised me most wasn't the technology; it was the shift in how I think about my role. I'm no longer just writing documentation. I'm designing systems that improve documentation. And I did this without writing complex code or becoming a full-time developer.

If you are a writer or someone from a non-technical background, this is my takeaway:

You don't need to “become a developer” to work with AI.
You just need to understand your problem deeply and let AI help you build around it.

 

Happy vibe-coding!

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2603
entry.content:
What do you do when a released assessment no longer reflects reality? That's the main topic of this month's release in SAP Asset Performance Management. We also have a very useful enhancement for directly accessing help within your workflow. With SAP Companion, you can now access contextual guidance, documentation, and explanations exactly where you need them — without leaving the application. Let's get into the details.

Reassess the risk and criticality of technical objects

The Challenge

In asset-intensive industries, change is constant.
Asset conditions evolve, maintenance strategies are refined, and new insights emerge over time.

Risk and Criticality Assessments are designed to reflect these realities — but until now, once an assessment was released, it could no longer be revisited.

This created a gap:

  • Assessments could become outdated as validity periods expired
  • Changes in templates or strategies were difficult to reflect
  • Users often had to recreate assessments from scratch
  • There was limited visibility into how risk evolved over time

The Solution

With the introduction of Reassessment with Versioning, you can now revise released assessments while maintaining full history.

Instead of replacing an assessment, the system creates a new version in Created status, while the currently released version remains active. This allows you to safely update assessments without disrupting ongoing operations.

During reassessment, the system supports you by:

  • Evaluating assigned technical objects and highlighting conflicts
  • Identifying objects that cannot be carried forward, such as those already part of ongoing assessments, inactive, or marked for deletion
  • Allowing you to decide which objects to carry forward
  • Highlighting obsolete templates and ensuring valid versions are used
  • Preserving role assignments for consistency

Reassessment of Risk and Criticality Assessment and Versioning

The Outcome

Reassessment with versioning brings flexibility and control to the assessment process.

You can now:

  • Keep assessments aligned with changing conditions and strategies
  • Maintain continuity without recreating assessments
  • Compare versions to track how risk evolves over time
  • Validate decisions and ensure consistency
  • Provide full traceability and auditability

Once the new version is released, the previous version is automatically marked as obsolete — ensuring a clear and structured lifecycle. For more information see the SAP Help Portal.

Overall, this helps ensure that your risk and criticality assessments remain accurate, transparent, and aligned with operational reality.

Help Where You Need It - Improvements to SAP Companion

You’re in the middle of a task, something isn’t clear, and suddenly you’re forced to stop — search documentation, open another tab, or dig through support material.

That interruption breaks your flow.

With the contextual help panel in the Fiori Launchpad, guidance is available directly within the application.

Users can access:

  • Contextual explanations
  • Documentation links
  • Technical component details

All without leaving their current workspace. For more information, see @Sivda's blog Get Help Where You Need it: SAP Companion in SAP Asset Performance Management and watch the video:

Click thumbnail to watch the video "Get Help Where You Need It - SAP Companion"

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • You can explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the latest update of the APM link collection. 

See you for the 2604 update!

entry.title: How BIE Generative AI in SAP Sales Cloud V2 Can Accelerate Sales Response, Quality, and Conversion
entry.content:
Process story and business flow

A quote request rarely arrives in a perfect format.

More often, it comes as an email with a mix of free text, item references, quantities, delivery expectations and so on. Sometimes there is also extra context hidden in the wording: this is for a shutdown window, this should follow an existing agreement, or this request is linked to a wider project.

That is usually where the work begins.

Before pricing, validation, or commercial alignment even starts, someone has to read the request, identify what matters, interpret the context, and turn all of that into something structured enough to create a sales quote. In more complex buying environments, this is not unusual - customers may use different interaction channels during their buying journey, which helps explain why inbound email quote requests often arrive fragmented across formats and touchpoints rather than as a single clean transaction.

A typical example could be this:

Please quote 24 units of valve kit VK-220 and 12 filter assemblies FA-90 for our Porto facility. We need delivery before the maintenance shutdown in week 39. Please align pricing with our existing agreement and see the attached sheet for the full list.

This is where Business Information Extraction (BIE) in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 becomes relevant.

SAP documents BIE as the capability used to configure extraction logic for AI-assisted creation of sales quotes from emails, allowing organizations to define the scenario that interprets the incoming request and supports quote creation. 

So, in simple terms, the flow could look like this:

 

What changes here is not that the seller disappears from the process. What changes is that the seller can start from a better first draft instead of spending unnecessary time decoding the request.

Business benefits

The value of Business Information Extraction (BIE) goes beyond data extraction itself.
Its real impact lies in how it streamlines and improves the entire quote process, from request intake to final quote creation.

Each benefit shown below reflects a direct improvement in the flow once extraction is embedded early in the process:

 

 

Hence, BIE doesn’t just extract data  - it stabilizes, accelerates, and adapts the entire quote process end-to-end.

Use case scenarios and the flexibility of elements and classes

This is where BIE becomes more interesting.

SAP’s configuration model is not just about extracting static fields. It is built around three design components:

  • Elements, which represent explicit values to be extracted - I.e for quotes: Product ID, Product Name, Quantity and unit, request date and custom elements tailored to specific business needs, such as customer reference numbers.
  • Classes, which represent values derived from the content - Delivery Priority and custom classes tailored to specific business needs, such as customer type, request category, product family, and so on.
  • As a result of combining both elements and classes - Flexible scenarios can be defined, bringing these components together into a structured, business-relevant use case that adapts the extraction logic to specific processes and requirements.
     

That means the email-to-quote use case can be adapted depending on how the business actually receives requests.

Therefore, a simple scenario may focus on the elements and classes:

  • Element: Product ID
  • Element: Product Name
  • Element: Quantity
  • Element; Requested Date
  • Classes: Delivery Priority (Urgent request, Right away)

and that alone can already reduce a lot of manual effort!

But many organizations will want to go further. For example, a more operational quote flow could include elements such as:

  • Customer material number
  • contract reference
  • asset or serial reference

And it could include classes such as:

  • request type: new product, replacement
  • delivery priority: urgent, standard, planned
  • commercial context: contract-linked, project-driven, ad hoc
  • product family: spare part, accessory, consumable, finished good

This is where the “configurable to each customer’s specific business scenario” message really matters. The same BIE capability can be adapted to a customer that mostly handles recurring replenishment requests, and also to one that works with more project-heavy, location-specific, or contract-sensitive quote motions. Hence, SAP Sales Cloud V2 administrators configure the scenario used for AI-assisted sales quotes from emails, which is what makes this flexibility operationally meaningful rather than just conceptual.

Value accelerator path for easy activation

Understanding the use case is important, but making it easy to activate is what turns the idea into something real. The value accelerator path below brings together the main prerequisites, configuration steps, and scenario design choices needed to enable Business Information Extraction for AI-assisted email-to-quote.

See further about the prerequisites and configuration steps HERE.

​Addressing Business KPIs and Measuring outcomes early

Activating the use case is only part of the story. The next step is showing that it is improving the quote process in a visible and measurable way.

A practical starting point is to focus on four simple KPIs:

  • Time from request receipt to quote draft
    to show whether the intake process is becoming faster
  • Manual effort per quote
    to understand whether sellers spend less time on repetitive data capture
  • Rework caused by intake errors
    to track whether avoidable corrections are going down
  • First-pass quote completeness and tailored to your business need
    to see whether the first version of the quote is stronger, more complete and aligned to your business requirement

Together, these indicators help connect the use case to real operational outcomes, not just technical activation.

The best approach is to keep the first measurement cycle simple: start with one bounded scenario, define a baseline, measure the early impact, and refine from there. That kind of phased activation is usually the fastest way to make the use case real without turning it into a long design exercise.

 

Final thoughts

Business Information Extraction for email-to-quote helps bridge a common gap in the quote process: the gap between how customers send requests and how sales teams need that information to exist inside CRM.

That is what makes the use case compelling. It is not just an AI feature layered on top of a sales process. It is a practical mechanism to reduce friction where friction already exists, at the point where unstructured demand first enters the business.

And that is also why the configurability matters so much. The real strength is not only that BIE can extract data from unstructured emails, but that it can be shaped around the customer’s own quote scenario through the right combination of elements, classes, and scenario design. SAP’s current documentation supports setting up the model directly for AI-assisted sales quote creation from emails.

For teams working with SAP Sales Cloud V2, this makes BIE a very credible use case to activate: practical, business-relevant, configurable, and measurable. 

Thanks for reading!

entry.title: Re: Intercompany return process steps and scope item
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the intercompany return process is not fully symmetrical between issuing and receiving plants. Scope item 2F4 (Intercompany Returns) extends BKP (Customer Returns) but is designed around the receiving plant. For an issuing plant that wants to return goods, you cannot directly use the IC supplier; instead, you must model the process via a standard vendor return PO (NB/NB2) with a “normal” supplier, while intercompany logic is handled through the sales side of the partner company.

Why you can’t use IC Supplier directly is because, IC suppliers are tied to intercompany sales/stock transfer processes, not vendor returns. SAP’s design expects the receiving company to trigger the return (customer return), not the issuing company. Using IC supplier in a return PO would break the standard integration logic.

So the recommended approach is

  • Issuing Plant → Use NB/NB2 return PO with a normal vendor.
  • Receiving Plant → Use 2F4 scope item to process the return as customer returns.
  • Master Data Setup →
    • Maintain vendor master for the “normal supplier.”
    • Ensure customer master exists for the intercompany partner.
    • Map plants to company codes correctly for FI postings.

entry.title: Join Now: SAP Signavio Regional Challenge 2026
entry.content:
Exciting news for our community! We’re happy to share another opportunity for you to explore SAP technology in practice.

The SAP Signavio Regional Challenge 2026 invites students and academic participants across Latin America and the Caribbean to step into the world of business process transformation.

At its core, SAP Signavio is a leading solution that enables organizations to model, analyze and optimize business processes in a collaborative and data-driven way. Through this challenge, participants will gain hands-on experience with the same tools used by global companies to improve efficiency, identify bottlenecks, and drive innovation.

But this is more than just a learning experience. Throughout the journey, participants will engage in expert-led sessions, build practical knowledge and even work toward SAP Signavio certification.

The contest is open to students, professors, and academic participants connected to institutions within the SAP University Alliances ecosystem. No prior experience with SAP Signavio is required — the contest is designed to support learning along the way.

 

Key Event Details

The SAP Signavio Challenge is a regional, fully online initiative that invites students and academic participants across Latin America and the Caribbean to explore how businesses can improve the way they operate.

Once registered, participants gain access to SAP Signavio environment, onboarding materials and are guided through each stage of the journey: from understanding process thinking to applying it in practice. 

At the center of the experience is a business challenge experience, where participants will:

  • Analyze a business process
  • Identify inefficiencies and improvement opportunities
  • Design optimized solutions using SAP Signavio
  • Present their results in a structured and impactful way

This journey provides a clear understanding of how data, processes and technology come together to drive better business decisions and outcomes.

Recognition and Outcomes: Participants who complete the contest will receive an official certificate of participation, recognizing their engagement and achievement throughout the challenge.

Beyond certification, this experience delivers real, tangible value. Participants will:

  • Gain hands-on experience with SAP Signavio, a leading solution used by global companies
  • Develop practical skills in process analysis, problem-solving, and business optimization
  • Learn how to approach challenges with a consulting mindset, from identifying inefficiencies to proposing data-driven solutions
  • Build a stronger professional profile with concrete experience 

 

Agenda Overview

The challenge unfolds across key milestones where each step builds toward your final solution:

  • April 22 | Final day to register
    Registration closes and the challenge officially begins.
  • April 27 | Understanding what’s shaping tomorrow’s businesses
    Gain insights into AI trends and how SAP Signavio is applied to solve real organizational challenges.
  • April 28–29 |  Hands-on learning
    Participate in interactive workshops on BPMN 2.0 and SAP Signavio.
  • May 22 | Final submission
    Submit your optimized (TO-BE) process and simulation results.
  • May 29 | Final presentations and results
    Participants will present their solutions during the Winners Challenge, where the final results will be announced.

 

How to Participate

Joining is simple:

  1. Register through the official SAP Signavio LAC Contest page
  2. Confirm your participation via email
  3. Access onboarding materials and tools
  4. Start developing your solution

⏳ Important: Make sure to register before April 22 to secure your spot.

 

This is your opportunity to move beyond theory and experience how real business transformation happens.

Register now and be part of the SAP Signavio Regional Challenge 2026.

entry.title: Re: Hello SAP Community! My name is Moshe Naveh and I'm really excited about the new journey ahead o
entry.content: I’m Vishwa Bharadhwaj, an engineering student trying to understand where I can actually create value with what I learn.I don’t have everything figured out yet. I know I don’t want a role where I just execute tasks without thinking. I want to work on problems that exist outside textbooks. Problems where the solution changes something real. Right now I’m drawn to the intersection of engineering and AI. I’ve seen how systems improve when data is used properly. I want to be part of building that. Not just studying it.I’ve noticed something about how I learn. When I see a concept applied in a real scenario, it sticks. That’s why areas like SAP, automation, and data started making more sense to me. I don’t enjoy learning in isolation. I prefer building, testing, and seeing outcomes.In the next few years, I want to become someone who can take a vague problem and turn it into a working solution. Not perfect. But useful. Something people can rely on.I’m still in the process of getting there.

entry.title: Re: SAP UI5 - Component-Based Architecture: Shell, Feature and Modules
entry.content:
Hey @wridgeu , good question!

To be honest I can only really speak for the frontend and architecture side here.
Backend design is handled by our backend developers and I don't want to claim things I can't fully back up.

What I can say is that our applications in the monorepo share some services and have dedicated ones for others. The services expose certain EntitySets and Actions for specific use cases, so I would not say they are fully generic. Maybe only in parts.

Whether the backend is truly decoupled from the concrete use case is probably a better question for the backend side. From a frontend perspective we just try to keep the input a module needs as minimal as possible, for example just a GUID, and let the module take care of the rest from there.

entry.title: How to use BTP Destination with REST API in UI5 App (BAS local development)
entry.content:
Hello,

I created a Destination in my SAP BTP subaccount (connection test is successful).
(see screenshot below)

 

My goal is to deploy the app later to Cloud Foundry.
However, first I want to make my UI5 app work locally in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) using the Destination, including authentication and data retrieval from the backend.

Current App Configuration:

ui5.yaml

# yaml-language-server: $schema=https://sap.github.io/ui5-tooling/schema/ui5.yaml.json

specVersion: "4.0"
metadata:
  name: testproject1
type: application

server:
  customMiddleware:
    - name: fiori-tools-proxy
      afterMiddleware: compression
      configuration:
        ignoreCertErrors: false
        ui5:
          path:
            - /resources
            - /test-resources
          url: https://ui5.sap.com
        backend:
          - path: /backend
            destination: TEST_BACKEND

    - name: fiori-tools-appreload
      afterMiddleware: compression
      configuration:
        port: 35729
        path: webapp
        delay: 300

    - name: fiori-tools-preview
      afterMiddleware: fiori-tools-appreload
      configuration:
        flp:
          theme: sap_horizon

xs-app.json

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/backend/(.*)$",
      "target": "/$1",
      "destination": "TEST_BACKEND",
      "authenticationType": "none"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/resources/$1",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "destination": "ui5"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/test-resources/(.*)$",
      "target": "/test-resources/$1",
      "authenticationType": "none",
      "destination": "ui5"
    },
    {
      "source": "^(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "html5-apps-repo-rt",
      "authenticationType": "xsuaa"
    }
  ]
}

AJAX in Controller

        _loadMetadata: async function () {              
            $.ajax({
                type: "GET",
                url: "/testproject1/backend/application/api/v1/documenthub/$metadata",
                headers: {
                    "Accept": "application/json"
                },
                success: function (oData) {
                    console.log("Success", oData);
                    MessageToast.show("Metadata erfolgreich geladen");
                },
                error: function (err) {
                    console.log("Error", err);
                    MessageBox.error("Fehler beim Laden von Metadata");
                }
            });

        }

Problem

testproject1 in AJAX-URL is my app-ID from manifest.
Case 1: 
When Calling 
url: "/testproject1/backend/application/api/v1/documenthub/$metadata"

I get 404

Case 2:
When Calling

url: "/backend/application/api/v1/documenthub/$metadata"

I get 500

Console log shows:

Additional Info:
- Destination "Check Connection" works in BTP
- Backend works in Postman with OAuth2
- Problem occurs only in BAS local preview

I have already watched some posts and questions on similar problems, but they didn't help me solve my problem.

I would be grateful if someone could help me.

Timur

entry.title: Missing Item Notes in Purchase Contract Output in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to share an issue we encountered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud where certain item texts were not appearing in the Purchase Contract output, even though they were maintained correctly. This may help others facing similar inconsistencies in text determination.

 

Issue:

In Purchase Contracts, the Info Record PO Text and Material PO Text were maintained and displayed in the document, but were missing in the generated output form.

After analyzing the affected items, we noticed that the missing texts were assigned the text status '*'. This status indicates that the text is available but not automatically adopted into the document output unless manually selected.

 

Root Cause:

The F1 help for the status pointed us to Customizing activity 104066 Define Copying Rules for Item Texts in Contracts. In our configuration, the copying rule for these text types was set to '*', meaning the text is displayed to the user, but must be manually adopted into the document if it should appear in the output.

 

Solution:

We adjusted the copying rule for the relevant text types from '*' to a blank value. After this change, the item texts were automatically adopted and appeared correctly in the Purchase Contract output without manual intervention.

 

Summary:

If you notice missing item notes in Purchase Contract output, it’s worth checking Customizing activity 104066 and verifying the copying rules for Material PO Text, Info Record PO Text, and any other relevant text types.

We were expecting an automatic adoption of the texts in Purchase Contract Output, however our customizing was set up for a manual adoption.

entry.title: Re: Validation of document item text does not work with a...
entry.content:
Hello @djwafelap

Given the explanation you provided on your client’s target, I would not recommend splitting the logic into “one rule for Create Supplier Invoice” and “one rule for all other apps” based on app or app ID precondition. The reason is because of the framework in the Journal Entry.

So, for your specific idea: Can we set a specific app or app ID as a precondition, and then work with two rules?

I would not recommend that. The framework field in Public Cloud is business context driven and not app-ID driven. That means what you are proposing is not reliable for your scenario. Considering your client wants to ensure that all final accounting lines carry a text, regardless of account type, then your original validation on DocumentItemText is conceptually actually the correct target field, because it checks the final accounting document item text, not just the UI entry field. See KBA 3066300 for more insights.

The lowest effort realistic design if your goal is one common enforcement rule, no maintenance of many field status groups and no dependency on posting key field status configuration, then I would recommend:

  • You keep one validation rule on DocumentItemText in the Journal Entry context
  • Add one substitution for the supplier invoice scenario to fill DocumentItemText for the lines that otherwise remain empty
  • Use header text or another business-defined source as the fallback text for those generated lines.

This will help you avoid trying to maintain 30 field status groups and avoids relying on posting key field status customisation.

On your question about posting key field status, in Public Cloud, you do not have the classic on premise posting key customisation pattern. That is why trying to solve this through posting key field status is not the direction I would suggest here. The cleaner Public Cloud approach is substitution plus validation.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Requirement – Credit Management for Cement Sales Orders
entry.content:
We have a requirement related to Credit Management in SAP S/4HANA RISE.

Requirement:
If a customer’s overdue is ≥ 90 days, the system should not allow creation of a Sales Order. This restriction should be applicable only for Cement Sales. For other materials, no change is required and the process should remain as is.

Current Business Scenario:

  • We are currently using credit management at the delivery level (Credit Group 02 – Delivery).
  • There is no restriction during Sales Order creation; all checks are applied at the time of delivery creation.
  • For oldest open items, a limit of 180 days is maintained, beyond which the system restricts processing.

New Requirement:
If the customer’s oldest open item is greater than 90 days, the system should not allow Sales Order creation specifically for Cement materials. For other materials (such as TMT, Wire Rod, etc.), the process should continue as per the current business.

Kindly suggest how this requirement can be fulfilled.
Also, please advise whether creating a separate order type for Cement Sales is required, as currently we are using the same order type for all domestic sales.

@Lakshmipathi Sir, please suggest & let me know if any additional info required from my end.

SD (Sales and Distribution) SD Billing 

Regards,

Soumya Nath.
